question:
Let $H$ be an $n \times n$ matrix all of whose entries are $\pm 1$ and
whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Suppose $H$ has an $a \times b$ submatrix
whose entries are all $1$. Show that $ab \leq n$.
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Choose a set of $a$ rows $r_1, \dots, r_a$
containing an $a \times b$ submatrix whose
entries are all 1. Then for $i,j \in\{1, \dots, a\}$, we have
$r_i \cdot r_j = n$ if $i=j$ and 0 otherwise. Hence
\[
\sum_{i,j=1}^a r_i \cdot r_j = an.
\]
On the other hand, the term on the left is the dot product of
$r_1 + \cdots + r_a$ with itself, i.e., its squared length. Since
this vector has $a$ in each of its first $b$ coordinates, the dot product
is at least $a^2 b$. Hence $an \geq a^2 b$,
whence $n \geq ab$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
Suppose without loss of generality that the $a \times b$ submatrix
occupies the first $a$ rows and the first $b$ columns.
Let $M$ be the submatrix occupying the first $a$ rows and the last
$n-b$ columns. Then the hypothesis implies that the matrix
$MM^T$ has $n-b$'s on the main diagonal and $-b$'s elsewhere.
Hence the column vector $v$ of length $a$ consisting of all 1's
satisfies $MM^T v = (n-ab)v$, so $n-ab$ is an eigenvalue of $MM^T$.
But $MM^T$ is semidefinite, so its eigenvalues are all nonnegative
real numbers. Hence $n-ab \geq 0$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
A matrix as in the problem is called a \emph{Hadamard matrix}, because
it meets the equality condition of Hadamard's inequality:
any $n \times n$ matrix with $\pm 1$ entries has absolute determinant
at most $n^{n/2}$, with equality if and only if the rows are mutually
orthogonal
(from the interpretation of the determinant as the volume of a paralellepiped
whose edges are parallel to the row vectors).
Note that this implies  that the columns are also mutually orthogonal.
A generalization of this problem, with a similar proof, is known
as \emph{Lindsey's lemma}: the sum of the entries in any
$a \times b$ submatrix of a Hadamard matrix is at most $\sqrt{abn}$.
Stanley notes that Ryser (1981) asked for the smallest size of a Hadamard
matrix containing an $r \times s$ submatrix of all 1's, and refers to
the URL \texttt{www3.interscience.wiley.com/cgi-bin/ abstract/110550861/ABSTRACT} for more information.

descriptive_long.question: Let $hadamardmatrix$ be a $dimension \times dimension$ matrix all of whose entries are $\pm 1$ and whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Suppose $hadamardmatrix$ has a $numrows \times numcols$ submatrix whose entries are all $1$. Show that $numrows numcols \leq dimension$.
descriptive_long.solution:
extbf{First solution:}
Choose a set of $numrows$ rows $firstrow, \dots, rowindexa$
containing a $numrows 	imes numcols$ submatrix whose
entries are all 1. Then for $indexi,indexj \in\{1, \dots, numrows\}$, we have
$rowindexi \cdot rowindexj = dimension$ if $indexi = indexj$ and $0$ otherwise. Hence
\[
\sum_{indexi,indexj=1}^{numrows} rowindexi \cdot rowindexj = numrows dimension.
\]
On the other hand, the term on the left is the dot product of
$firstrow + \cdots + rowindexa$ with itself, i.e., its squared length. Since
this vector has $numrows$ in each of its first $numcols$ coordinates, the dot product
is at least $numrows^{2} numcols$. Hence $numrows dimension \geq numrows^{2} numcols$,
whence $dimension \geq numrows numcols$ as desired.

	extbf{Second solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
Suppose without loss of generality that the $numrows 	imes numcols$ submatrix
occupies the first $numrows$ rows and the first $numcols$ columns.
Let $remainderblock$ be the submatrix occupying the first $numrows$ rows and the last
$dimension - numcols$ columns. Then the hypothesis implies that the matrix
$remainderblock\,remainderblock^{T}$ has $(dimension - numcols)$'s on the main diagonal and $-numcols$'s elsewhere.
Hence the column vector $onesvector$ of length $numrows$ consisting of all $1$'s
satisfies $remainderblock\,remainderblock^{T} onesvector = (dimension - numrows numcols) onesvector$, so $dimension - numrows numcols$ is an eigenvalue of $remainderblock\,remainderblock^{T}$.
But $remainderblock\,remainderblock^{T}$ is semidefinite, so its eigenvalues are all nonnegative
real numbers. Hence $dimension - numrows numcols \ge 0$.

	extbf{Remarks:}
A matrix as in the problem is called a \emph{Hadamard matrix}, because
it meets the equality condition of Hadamard's inequality:
any $dimension 	imes dimension$ matrix with $\pm 1$ entries has absolute determinant
at most $dimension^{dimension/2}$, with equality if and only if the rows are mutually
orthogonal (from the interpretation of the determinant as the volume of a parallelepiped
whose edges are parallel to the row vectors).
Note that this implies that the columns are also mutually orthogonal.
A generalization of this problem, with a similar proof, is known
as \emph{Lindsey's lemma}: the sum of the entries in any
$numrows 	imes numcols$ submatrix of a Hadamard matrix is at most $\sqrt{numrows numcols dimension}$.
Stanley notes that Ryser (1981) asked for the smallest size of a Hadamard
matrix containing an $r 	imes subsize$ submatrix of all $1$'s, and refers to
the URL 	exttt{www3.interscience.wiley.com/cgi-bin/ abstract/110550861/ABSTRACT} for more information.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $butterfly$ be an $pineapple \times pineapple$ matrix all of whose entries are $\pm 1$ and
whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Suppose $butterfly$ has an $waterfall \times spaceship$ submatrix
whose entries are all $1$. Show that $waterfall\;spaceship \leq pineapple$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Choose a set of $waterfall$ rows $tangerine, \dots, scarecrow$
containing an $waterfall \times spaceship$ submatrix whose
entries are all 1. Then for $pinecones,saxophone \in\{1, \dots, waterfall\}$, we have
$marathon \cdot alligators = pineapple$ if $pinecones=saxophone$ and 0 otherwise. Hence
\[
\sum_{pinecones,saxophone=1}^{waterfall} marathon \cdot alligators = waterfall\,pineapple.
\]
On the other hand, the term on the left is the dot product of
$tangerine + \cdots + scarecrow$ with itself, i.e., its squared length. Since
this vector has $waterfall$ in each of its first $spaceship$ coordinates, the dot product
is at least $waterfall^2\,spaceship$. Hence $waterfall\,pineapple \geq waterfall^2\,spaceship$,
whence $pineapple \geq waterfall\;spaceship$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
Suppose without loss of generality that the $waterfall \times spaceship$ submatrix
occupies the first $waterfall$ rows and the first $spaceship$ columns.
Let $chocolate$ be the submatrix occupying the first $waterfall$ rows and the last
$pineapple-spaceship$ columns. Then the hypothesis implies that the matrix
$chocolate choco late^T$ has $pineapple-spaceship$'s on the main diagonal and $-spaceship$'s elsewhere.
Hence the column vector $lighthouse$ of length $waterfall$ consisting of all 1's
satisfies $chocolate chocolate^T lighthouse = (pineapple-waterfall\;spaceship)lighthouse$, so $pineapple-waterfall\;spaceship$ is an eigenvalue of $chocolate chocolate^T$.
But $chocolate chocolate^T$ is semidefinite, so its eigenvalues are all nonnegative
real numbers. Hence $pineapple-waterfall\;spaceship \geq 0$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
A matrix as in the problem is called a \emph{Hadamard matrix}, because
it meets the equality condition of Hadamard's inequality:
any $pineapple \times pineapple$ matrix with $\pm 1$ entries has absolute determinant
at most $pineapple^{pineapple/2}$, with equality if and only if the rows are mutually
orthogonal
(from the interpretation of the determinant as the volume of a paralellepiped
whose edges are parallel to the row vectors).
Note that this implies  that the columns are also mutually orthogonal.
A generalization of this problem, with a similar proof, is known
as \emph{Lindsey's lemma}: the sum of the entries in any
$waterfall \times spaceship$ submatrix of a Hadamard matrix is at most $\sqrt{waterfall\;spaceship\;pineapple}$.
Stanley notes that Ryser (1981) asked for the smallest size of a Hadamard
matrix containing an $r \times backpacks$ submatrix of all 1's, and refers to
the URL \texttt{www3.interscience.wiley.com/cgi-bin/ abstract/110550861/ABSTRACT} for more information.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $nonorthmatrix$ be an $minilength \times minilength$ matrix all of whose entries are $\pm 1$ and
whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Suppose $nonorthmatrix$ has a $columncount \times rowcount$ submatrix
whose entries are all $1$. Show that $columncount rowcount \leq minilength$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Choose a set of $columncount$ rows $columnprime, \dots, columnalpha$
containing a $columncount \times rowcount$ submatrix whose
entries are all 1. Then for $endpoint,startpoint \in\{1, \dots, columncount\}$, we have
$columnith \cdot columnjay = minilength$ if $endpoint=startpoint$ and 0 otherwise. Hence
\[
\sum_{endpoint,startpoint=1}^{columncount} columnith \cdot columnjay = columncount minilength.
\]
On the other hand, the term on the left is the dot product of
$columnprime + \cdots + columnalpha$ with itself, i.e., its squared length. Since
this vector has $columncount$ in each of its first $rowcount$ coordinates, the dot product
is at least $columncount^2 rowcount$. Hence $columncount minilength \geq columncount^2 rowcount$,
whence $minilength \geq columncount rowcount$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
Suppose without loss of generality that the $columncount \times rowcount$ submatrix
occupies the first $columncount$ rows and the first $rowcount$ columns.
Let $supermatrix$ be the submatrix occupying the first $columncount$ rows and the last
$minilength-rowcount$ columns. Then the hypothesis implies that the matrix
$supermatrix supermatrix^T$ has $minilength-rowcount$'s on the main diagonal and $-rowcount$'s elsewhere.
Hence the column vector $nullvector$ of length $columncount$ consisting of all 1's
satisfies $supermatrix supermatrix^T nullvector = (minilength-columncount rowcount)nullvector$, so $minilength-columncount rowcount$ is an eigenvalue of $supermatrix supermatrix^T$.
But $supermatrix supermatrix^T$ is semidefinite, so its eigenvalues are all nonnegative
real numbers. Hence $minilength-columncount rowcount \geq 0$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
A matrix as in the problem is called a \emph{Hadamard matrix}, because
it meets the equality condition of Hadamard's inequality:
any $minilength \times minilength$ matrix with $\pm 1$ entries has absolute determinant
at most $minilength^{minilength/2}$, with equality if and only if the rows are mutually
orthogonal
(from the interpretation of the determinant as the volume of a paralellepiped
whose edges are parallel to the row vectors).
Note that this implies  that the columns are also mutually orthogonal.
A generalization of this problem, with a similar proof, is known
as \emph{Lindsey's lemma}: the sum of the entries in any
$columncount \times rowcount$ submatrix of a Hadamard matrix is at most $\sqrt{columncount rowcount minilength}$.
Stanley notes that Ryser (1981) asked for the smallest size of a Hadamard
matrix containing an $r \times antiscale$ submatrix of all 1's, and refers to
the URL \texttt{www3.interscience.wiley.com/cgi-bin/ abstract/110550861/ABSTRACT} for more information.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qarpsinl$ be an $heskoudr \times heskoudr$ matrix all of whose entries are $\pm 1$ and
whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Suppose $qarpsinl$ has a $ptfewyvn \times ulmragci$ submatrix
whose entries are all $1$. Show that $ptfewyvn ulmragci \leq heskoudr$.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Choose a set of $ptfewyvn$ rows $vjtwqrsa, \dots, ygnobrca$
containing a $ptfewyvn \times ulmragci$ submatrix whose
entries are all 1. Then for $jqyrsemb,bxtlauhq \in\{1, \dots, ptfewyvn\}$, we have
$kxpqvntu \cdot dhzmltfe = heskoudr$ if $jqyrsemb=bxtlauhq$ and 0 otherwise. Hence
\[
\sum_{jqyrsemb,bxtlauhq=1}^{ptfewyvn} kxpqvntu \cdot dhzmltfe = ptfewyvn heskoudr.
\]
On the other hand, the term on the left is the dot product of
$vjtwqrsa + \cdots + ygnobrca$ with itself, i.e., its squared length. Since
this vector has $ptfewyvn$ in each of its first $ulmragci$ coordinates, the dot product
is at least $ptfewyvn^2 \, ulmragci$. Hence $ptfewyvn heskoudr \geq ptfewyvn^2 \, ulmragci$,
whence $heskoudr \geq ptfewyvn ulmragci$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
Suppose without loss of generality that the $ptfewyvn \times ulmragci$ submatrix
occupies the first $ptfewyvn$ rows and the first $ulmragci$ columns.
Let $zfurikpa$ be the submatrix occupying the first $ptfewyvn$ rows and the last
$heskoudr-ulmragci$ columns. Then the hypothesis implies that the matrix
$zfurikpa zfurikpa^{T}$ has $heskoudr-ulmragci$'s on the main diagonal and $-ulmragci$'s elsewhere.
Hence the column vector $oxmdqsei$ of length $ptfewyvn$ consisting of all 1's
satisfies $zfurikpa zfurikpa^{T} \, oxmdqsei = (heskoudr-ptfewyvn ulmragci)\, oxmdqsei$, so $heskoudr-ptfewyvn ulmragci$ is an eigenvalue of $zfurikpa zfurikpa^{T}$.
But $zfurikpa zfurikpa^{T}$ is semidefinite, so its eigenvalues are all nonnegative
real numbers. Hence $heskoudr-ptfewyvn ulmragci \geq 0$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
A matrix as in the problem is called a \emph{Hadamard matrix}, because
it meets the equality condition of Hadamard's inequality:
any $heskoudr \times heskoudr$ matrix with $\pm 1$ entries has absolute determinant
at most $heskoudr^{heskoudr/2}$, with equality if and only if the rows are mutually
orthogonal
(from the interpretation of the determinant as the volume of a paralellepiped
whose edges are parallel to the row vectors).
Note that this implies  that the columns are also mutually orthogonal.
A generalization of this problem, with a similar proof, is known
as \emph{Lindsey's lemma}: the sum of the entries in any
$ptfewyvn \times ulmragci$ submatrix of a Hadamard matrix is at most $\sqrt{ptfewyvn ulmragci heskoudr}$.
Stanley notes that Ryser (1981) asked for the smallest size of a Hadamard
matrix containing an $r \times cmrqtnvo$ submatrix of all 1's, and refers to
the URL \texttt{www3.interscience.wiley.com/cgi-bin/ abstract/110550861/ABSTRACT} for more information.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix a non-zero real number $c$.  Let  

\[
H=(h_{ij})_{1\le i\le k,\;1\le j\le n}, \qquad k\le n,\qquad h_{ij}\in\{+c,\,-c\},
\]

be a $k\times n$ matrix whose rows are pairwise orthogonal with respect to the usual Euclidean inner product on $\mathbb R^{\,n}$, so that  

\[
HH^{\mathsf T}=n\,c^{2}I_k. \tag{1}
\]

For index-sets $R\subseteq\{1,\dots ,k\}$ and $C\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}$ write $a:=|R|$, $b:=|C|$, and denote by  

\[
A:=H[R,C]
\]

the $a\times b$ sub-matrix formed by the rows in $R$ and the columns in $C$.

1.  (Spectral bound)  
    Prove that the spectral norm (largest singular value) of $A$ satisfies  

    \[
        \lVert A\rVert_2\le |c|\sqrt n. \tag{$\star$}
    \]

2.  (Low-rank sub-matrices)  
    Suppose that the rank of $A$ equals $r$ ($1\le r\le\min\{a,b\}$).  Show that  

    \[
        ab\le r\,n. \tag{$\star\!\star$}
    \]

3.  (Lindsey's inequality recovered)  
    Deduce from $(\star\!\star)$ that \emph{if every entry of $A$ equals the same sign-choice of $c$} (so $A$ is a constant matrix, hence of rank $1$) then necessarily  

    \[
        ab\le n.
    \]
kernel_variant.solution:
Preliminaries.  
Because each entry of $H$ has absolute value $|c|$, every row of $H$ has Euclidean length $|c|\sqrt n$.  From (1) we also know that every non-zero singular value of $H$ equals $|c|\sqrt n$; hence the operator (spectral) norm of $H$ is  

\[
\lVert H\rVert_2=|c|\sqrt n. \tag{2}
\]

Let $S_R$ denote the $a\times k$ row-selector matrix that extracts rows in $R$, and let $P_C$ be the $n\times b$ column-selector matrix that extracts columns in $C$.  Then  

\[
A=S_RHP_C. \tag{3}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------------  
1.  Proof of $(\star)$.  
For any $x\in\mathbb R^{\,b}$ extend it to $\bar x\in\mathbb R^{\,n}$ by inserting zeros outside $C$, so $P_Cx=\bar x$.  Then  

\[
Ax=S_RH\bar x. \tag{4}
\]

Taking Euclidean norms and using (2),

\[
\lVert Ax\rVert_2\le\lVert S_R\rVert_2\,\lVert H\rVert_2\,\lVert\bar x\rVert_2
           \le 1\cdot |c|\sqrt n\cdot\lVert x\rVert_2,
\]

because $S_R$ merely deletes rows and therefore has operator norm $1$.  Hence  

\[
\lVert A\rVert_2=\sup_{x\neq 0}\frac{\lVert Ax\rVert_2}{\lVert x\rVert_2}\le |c|\sqrt n.
\quad\blacksquare
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------------  
2.  Proof of $(\star\!\star)$.  
Let the non-zero singular values of $A$ be $\sigma_1,\dots ,\sigma_r$.  By $(\star)$,  

\[
\sigma_i\le |c|\sqrt n\quad\text{for every }i. \tag{5}
\]

Because the Frobenius norm equals the $\ell^2$-sum of the singular values,  

\[
\lVert A\rVert_F^{\,2}=\sigma_1^{2}+\dots +\sigma_r^{2}. \tag{6}
\]

Since every entry of $A$ equals $\pm c$, there are $ab$ such entries, each of magnitude $|c|$, so  

\[
\lVert A\rVert_F^{\,2}=ab\,c^{2}. \tag{7}
\]

Combining (5)-(7) gives  

\[
ab\,c^{2}=\sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_i^{2}\le r\,(|c|\sqrt n)^{2}=r\,|c|^{2}n,
\]

and division by $|c|^{2}>0$ yields  

\[
ab\le r\,n.
\quad\blacksquare
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.  Lindsey's bound as a corollary.  
Suppose every entry of $A$ equals the same sign-choice of $c$.  Then $A$ is a constant matrix and therefore has rank $1$.  Taking $r=1$ in $(\star\!\star)$ gives  

\[
ab\le n,
\]

which is precisely Lindsey's inequality.  Note that we used only the implication ``constant $\Longrightarrow$ rank $1$''; the converse is \emph{not} asserted.  
\hfill$\blacksquare$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€  
â€˘  The original problem dealt only with rank-one (constant) sub-matrices and could be settled by elementary vector arithmetic.  
â€˘  The current kernel variant already required recognising Lindseyâ€™s inequality but still stayed within basic Cauchyâ€“Schwarz arguments.  

The enhanced variant is markedly harder because  
1.  It asks for a bound on the spectral norm of an arbitrary sub-matrix A; this forces the competitor to introduce operator norms, singular values, and their interaction with orthogonality outside the chosen rows and columns.  
2.  Part 2 replaces the â€śall-onesâ€™â€™ hypothesis by the far more general low-rank condition.  The solver must understand how the entire singular-value spectrum of A is constrained by HHáµ€ = n cÂ˛ I_k, and must relate the Frobenius and spectral norms to derive ab â‰¤ r n.  
3.  Lindseyâ€™s inequality now appears only as the easiest special case (r = 1); proving it is no longer the goal but a by-product of a broader, more sophisticated statement.  

Thus the enhanced variant introduces higher-order linear-algebraic notions (spectral norm, singular values, Frobenius norm), demands chaining several non-trivial inequalities, and generalises the original statement from rank 1 to arbitrary rank r, all of which substantially increase both conceptual depth and technical complexity.
